title: Cat Bump on Eyelid Margin: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Notice a growth on your cat's eyelid? Learn about common causes, when to see a vet, and why a photo is essential for triage. Urgency level: Medium.
content_html:
What is the bump on my cat's eyelid?

A growth appearing on the margin (the edge) of your cat's eyelid is often related to the Meibomian glands, which produce the oily layer of the tear film. These growths can be benign tumors, cysts, or inflammatory reactions like a chalazion. While less common than in dogs, feline eyelid tumors can sometimes be more locally aggressive, making early identification important.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for an eyelid bump is typically Medium. It is not usually a life-threatening emergency, but it requires a veterinary appointment within a few days. If you notice your cat squinting, excessive tearing, yellow discharge, or if they are pawing at the eye, the urgency increases. A growth that rubs against the surface of the eye can quickly cause a painful corneal ulcer.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, high-resolution photo is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. It allows a professional to see exactly where the mass is positioned. A photo helps determine if the bump is likely a fluid-filled cyst or a solid mass and whether it is currently touching the eyeball. This visual information is vital for deciding how quickly your cat needs to be seen and what diagnostic steps may be required.

faqs.question: Should I try to pop the bump if it looks like a pimple?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze or pop a growth near a cat's eye. This can cause severe pain, spread potential infection, or lead to a serious injury to the eye itself.

faqs.question: Will my cat need surgery for an eyelid bump?
faqs.answer: It depends on the type of growth. Some cysts may be managed with medication, but many eyelid tumors require surgical removal (often a wedge resection) to prevent them from irritating the cornea.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops to reduce the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Many human eye drops contain ingredients that are toxic or irritating to cats. Only use ophthalmic medications specifically prescribed by your veterinarian.
